title: Young adults are poor despite every metric which suggests otherwise
excerpt: 307 1,463 8,119 4,113,126 A recent article from The Cut received a huge amount of attention: ‘It’s Hard to See My Parents Live So Lavishly While We’re Struggling’. The piece includes vignettes of Millenial hardship: …

A recent article from The Cut received a huge amount of attention: ‘It’s Hard to See My Parents Live So Lavishly While We’re Struggling’. The piece includes vignettes of Millenial hardship:

…his dad, Steve, sold his company to a private equity firm about five years ago, which enabled him to retire comfortably in his mid-60s…Two years ago, Joe asked his dad for a loan. He wanted to start a lawn-care business and needed capital for commercial-grade equipment and a trailer, at least $15,000. He presented Steve with his business plan, which included a schedule to pay back the money. Still, Steve said “no.”…

Joe hasn’t started the business. He works for a property-management firm and does landscaping work on the side to bring in extra money. But he has two young kids of his own and finds it difficult to save. “Without some initial help, it’s pretty unrealistic that I’ll ever be able to go the entrepreneurial route,” he says.

Steve, on the other hand, thinks he’s teaching his son a valuable lesson on self-reliance. “It’s not that I can’t afford to help him,” Steve told me. “I just have a real problem with handouts. And I want him to have the satisfaction of knowing he’s built something himself, with his own hard work. That’s how I was raised, too.

The virality of this piece prompted a discussion between Louise Perry and Rob Henderson: ‘The politics of the downwardly mobile class’.

Rob disagreed with the Cut article’s perspective:

The way that this article is written, it’s intended, I think, to convey that millennials are really struggling… but, generally, Millennials are doing just fine. And even the link to the US GAO that they cite says that Millennial households were more likely than other generations to be college educated… incomes have remained flat across the three generations… There was an article a couple of years ago from Jean Twenge in The Atlantic showing that Millennials are doing just as well as previous generations were.

I disagree! I think that Millennials and Zoomers are not doing fine. In fact, I think there have been structural changes to our economy and society which clearly explain why these cohorts are failing to move through the five pillars of a stable middle-class existence: education, stable employment, marriage, homeownership, children.

Some of these negative changes stand out in the normal datasets, while some do not, for reasons I will explain. In short, what has happened is that previous generations were able to take advantage of highly valuable and productive social capital as well as their exploding financial capital to move through each of these life stages in a fiscally efficient manner. (Don’t worry: I will define what I mean by social capital and back my argument with data).

Our current young, lacking access to this social capital, must engage in enormous outlays of purely financial capital in order to achieve the same levels of stability and accomplishment — capital that they do not have. We see this clearly in the data on schooling, homeownership, and family formation.

All of this is hidden from the top-line metrics, usually in one of two ways:

  1. Huge increases in the cost of essential aspects of family life (homeownership, education, etc.) being offset by huge decreases in the cost of non-essential goods (televisions, phones, etc.) making the young appear wealthy in the abstract but without them actually being able to afford anything meaningful;
  2. By presenting false equivalencies which imply a level of choice that younger generations do not actually have. For example: an American family in 1975 could send their children to public school on the assumption that the vast majority of other children would belong to intact families, communities like their own, and would speak English as a first language. Now, realistically, many parents must turn to private schooling for the same reassurances.

On the surface it does seem as if the young are fine. Real median incomes are higher than they were for their parents at the same age, unemployment has spent most of the past decade at historic lows, and purchasing power has risen roughly 63 percent since 1973. Televisions, clothing, food, and air travel are cheaper than ever.

And yet this generation is also not marrying, not buying homes, not having children, and seem pretty miserable. I think we’re clearly in the midst of a tremendous measurement failure.

My argument is that previous generations received an enormous stock of social capital: trusted neighbors, functional public schools, a productive courtship culture, predictable career arcs, and a public square in which children could roam and adults could be relied upon. That stock, once given for free, has now been substantially liquidated.

Instead, the young must now buy back, item by item and at retail prices, what their grandparents received as a bounty of prior civilizational investments. The young must do so out of incomes that rose modestly while the prices of the essential elements of life rose radically. Price indexes measure the individual cost of discrete goods, but they are not intended to convey the total cost of personally repurchasing a destroyed commons.

This type of failure is well understood as a threat in economics. The old joke is that when a man marries his cleaner, the GDP of both households collapses even while the actual labor being done remains the same and everyone is better off. In our case, we’re seeing the opposite: a thousand small divorces and social fragmentations which boost the appearance of GDP but leave everyone poorer in reality.

Occasionally, researchers succeed in capturing and modelling these hidden transitions, and we get a glimpse into the deep faultlines under society — direct evidence that official measures miss what households actually feel. In 2024, for example, a compelling study by Lawrence Summers and colleagues showed that the gap between depressed consumer sentiment and cheerful official statistics closes once borrowing costs (excluded from the modern CPI but important for family finances) are counted as part of the cost of living. The consumers were right.

What actually got cheaper over the past fifty years? Electronics, entertainment, fast fashion, processed food, toys, screens of every kind. And what got more expensive, usually by many multiples? Housing, education, childcare, healthcare, insurance.

Absurdly, both of these movements register in the statistics as progress: one shows up as asset appreciation and the other as consumer surplus. But the lived reality for families feels like a pincer.

Housing is the least ambiguous case. At age 30, 55 percent of the Silent Generation owned homes. For Boomers the figure was 48 percent, for Gen X 42 percent, and for Millennials 33 percent. The national ratio of median home price to median household income, which stood around 3.2 through the 1990s, reached 5.0 in 2024, nearly matching its all-time high. The median age of the first-time buyer, 29 in the early 1980s, hit a record 40 in 2025. Whatever supposed wage gains the young have enjoyed, housing claws most of them back before they can be converted into anything durable.

Significant changes in rent have made this worse. The standard escape from renting is saving for a down payment, but soaring rent has made saving near impossible. Roughly half of all renter households now spend more than 30 percent of their income on rent and utilities, the federal threshold for being “cost-burdened,” and more than a quarter spend over half. Layer exploding student debt on top — of which more below — and you get a class of people in permanent limbo.

One rebuttal you hear to this predicament concerns square footage. The line is that due to consumer preference, houses today are larger than the bungalows of 1955, so price per square foot has risen less than price per house; the young, the argument goes, are demanding more luxury and are pricing themselves out of the market.

This is unconvincing on two counts. First, the market barely offers the smaller option: zoning regimes across the country make it easier to build large houses than small ones, and buyers who would prefer modest homes in decent areas frequently cannot find them. Scott Alexander described living alone in a three-bedroom house in Michigan because no acceptable one-bedroom existed near his workplace and the apartments were (his words!) ‘loud and crime-y’.

I think this latter point is a generalizable pattern. As Mr. Alexander discovered, the added, expensive square footage — like private school — is a hedge against public dysfunction and is another example of a replacement purchase. The over-priced neighborhood is perversely desirable when pricing is the only legal means of discrimination in an increasingly dysfunctional society.

Free childhoods disappeared alongside the loss of something harder to measure: the trust that makes a child outdoors seem normal rather than negligent and the local conditions that once made such trust reasonable. These conditions - like many other kinds of social integrity such as intact two-parent homes - have become aggressively sorted by class…

The childhood depicted in nostalgic media rested on a dense web of adults who knew each other, shared a rough moral sense, and could be relied upon to mind each other’s children. That web is now a feature of particular places (often expensive places) rather than a general inheritance, and discerning which places have kept it is of key importance for families hoping to raise agentic children with deep networks of trusted friends.

Perversely, this turns ‘overpriced homes’ into a feature, rather than a bug. When families have no organic access to likeminded, competent, stable, trustworthy families, they use one of the only mechanisms left which forces some kind of homogeneity: money. People’s revealed preference is to live in arbitrarily expensive neighborhoods because the cost is a moat against endemic dysfunction. Or, as one X user put it, families must escape the tyranny of the bottom quintile which has been allowed to run rampant.
— Childhood freedom is a now luxury good

The socializing that happened in parks, neighborhoods, boy scouts, etc. now must happen in the private domain. A bigger house, which includes a large playroom and yard, is the private replacement for a public square that no longer functions and is thus necessary infrastructure as much as luxury.

An owned home is the material base of psychological stability: it fixes housing costs against inflation, converts rent into equity, roots a family in a particular place among particular neighbors, and provides the security without which people demonstrably hesitate to marry and reproduce.

Robert Sampson’s research on Chicago found that the collective efficacy which keeps neighborhoods safe — mutual trust plus the willingness of residents to intervene for the common good — is strongest where residents own their homes and stay put. Ownership is both a private good and the raw material of the social capital whose destruction started this spiral. A generation locked out of ownership is locked out of both.

Marriage follows the same pattern. Women now substantially outnumber men on university campuses and outpace them in degrees earned, yet the preference for husbands who match or exceed a wife’s income and education has not correspondingly relaxed. The result is a radical market mismatch.

Daniel Lichter, Joseph Price, and Jeffrey Swigert constructed statistical profiles of the husbands that today’s unmarried women would marry, based on the actual husbands of demographically comparable married women. These “synthetic husbands” have incomes 58 percent higher than the real unmarried men available; they are 30 percent more likely to be employed and 19 percent more likely to hold a college degree. The men whom women are prepared to marry bear little resemblance to their struggling real-world counterparts. The few who shape up to the fantasy are snapped up quickly.

For a regular man, this implies that becoming marriageable now requires clearing exceptional bars: a degree (with the debt that comes attached) and an income well above the male median (also — 6ft, muscular physique, etc. etc.). Likewise social media has raised the expectations against which men are measured to make-believe levels. A working man in 1965 was marriageable by default in a way that his grandson is not. Again, all of this leverages tremendous economic pressure on men to overperform.

Supposing marriage and family formation is achieved, new parents discover that the activities formerly provided by ‘free-range childhoods’ must now be bought as expensive private services. Supervision that neighbors and older siblings once provided becomes daycare and after-school programs.

The average annual cost of childcare reached $13,128 in 2024, a 29 percent rise in four years; infant care now exceeds in-state college tuition in most of the US. All of this buys back, imperfectly and at great cost, what the 1960s family got free. “Intensive parenting,” routinely diagnosed as a cultural fashion, is a rational insurance response to a collapsed commons.

The transition to the economic default of the two-earner household intensifies these pressures. A household with a parent at home produces an enormous stream of untaxed, uncounted output: childcare, cooking and cleaning, budget management, and the daily maintenance of relations with other families (ie. social capital).

Move the at-home spouse into the labor market and every service she provided must be repurchased from the latest private equity roll-up (nursery, takeaway, cleaner, security system…). GDP records each purchase as growth. A family can thus be measured as substantially richer while consuming the same services — only now they spend less time together.

Education is the same. The credential became increasingly mandatory and increasingly ruinous. This is where Rob Henderson’s analysis really misses the mark: if a hugely increased percentage of young people are going to college — and thus losing years of their lives and accruing record debt — without substantially increased earnings, that bodes very badly for the young indeed.

In a service economy with proliferating occupational licensing and degree screening, the bachelor’s degree is now required in almost all dynamic sectors. I used to work for a company whose founder made a lot of noise about not requiring degrees! In practice, based on my experience both being interviewed and later interviewing and hiring others, the company absolutely did require degrees (in fact, it required the very best ones and prestigious graduate studies were desirable).

Real tuition and fees have roughly tripled at public universities since 1990 and risen about fivefold since 1970; average debt at graduation has risen 41 percent in real terms just since 2007, and total student debt now approaches $1.8 trillion. For a large fraction of borrowers the wage premium never offsets the balance, and the debt functions purely as a tax on family formation: it raises the savings threshold for a down payment, delays marriage, and delays or forecloses children.

And beneath higher education sits the same repurchasing dynamic at the school level. The public school was among the most valuable components of the inherited commons: perhaps not perfect, but usually a free, orderly, locally trusted institution that a family got with its address. Where it has decayed into disorder — and urban district performance and safety data leave little doubt that in many places it has — families face tough choices.

The cheap and effective Catholic school system of old has largely collapsed due to the dearth of nuns. And so the options are pay private tuition (which has risen about 158 percent in real terms since 1973), pay the housing premium for a catchment with a functional school, or pay in parental labor through homeschooling and forfeit the second income on which the broader economy is calibrated. The “good school district” premium is a particularly pure example of social capital being financialized and resold as real estate.

Employment volatility and changes in the career market compound all of this. Young people whose income arrives in unpredictable tranches rationally desire a larger private buffer before committing (savings, credentials, attainment of a rare stable career…). This comes at the moment when housing, education, and childcare inflation have made this buffer tremendously hard to attain.

The young are told they are richer, and in the currency of flat-screen televisions they are. But the goods that constitute a life — house, spouse, children, good school, good neighbors — have inflated so far beyond wages that the ordinary life path of 1965 now requires an extraordinary income. It turns out that we do actually need social capital. They are not doing well!

If you found this essay valuable, please share it. Want to read more? Get my best work, my book 'Leaving a Legacy: Inheritance, Charity, & Thousand-Year Families'.

- Johann

sentences.zh: 他向史蒂夫提出了他的商业计划，其中包括还款时间表。

sentences.en: Still, Steve said “no.”…
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，史蒂夫还是说“不”。

sentences.en: Joe hasn’t started the business.
sentences.zh: 乔还没有开始创业。

sentences.en: He works for a property-management firm and does landscaping work on the side to bring in extra money.
sentences.zh: 他在一家物业管理公司工作，并兼职做园林绿化工作以赚取额外收入。

sentences.en: But he has two young kids of his own and finds it difficult to save.
sentences.zh: 但他自己有两个年幼的孩子，很难挽救。

sentences.en: “Without some initial help, it’s pretty unrealistic that I’ll ever be able to go the entrepreneurial route,” he says.
sentences.zh: “如果没有一些初步的帮助，我想要走上创业之路是相当不现实的，”他说。

sentences.en: Steve, on the other hand, thinks he’s teaching his son a valuable lesson on self-reliance.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，史蒂夫认为他正在给儿子上一堂关于自力更生的宝贵课。

sentences.en: “It’s not that I can’t afford to help him,” Steve told me.
sentences.zh: “并不是我无力帮助他，”史蒂夫告诉我。

sentences.en: “I just have a real problem with handouts.
sentences.zh: “我只是对施舍有一个真正的问题。

sentences.en: And I want him to have the satisfaction of knowing he’s built something himself, with his own hard work.
sentences.zh: 我希望他能感到满足，因为他知道自己通过自己的努力创造了一些东西。

sentences.en: That’s how I was raised, too.
sentences.zh: 我也是这样长大的。

blocks.html_en: Joe hasn’t started the business. He works for a property-management firm and does landscaping work on the side to bring in extra money. But he has two young kids of his own and finds it difficult to save. “Without some initial help, it’s pretty unrealistic that I’ll ever be able to go the entrepreneurial route,” he says.

sentences.en: Joe hasn’t started the business.
sentences.zh: 乔还没有开始创业。

sentences.en: He works for a property-management firm and does landscaping work on the side to bring in extra money.
sentences.zh: 他在一家物业管理公司工作，并兼职做园林绿化工作以赚取额外收入。

sentences.en: But he has two young kids of his own and finds it difficult to save.
sentences.zh: 但他自己有两个年幼的孩子，很难挽救。

sentences.en: “Without some initial help, it’s pretty unrealistic that I’ll ever be able to go the entrepreneurial route,” he says.
sentences.zh: “如果没有一些初步的帮助，我想要走上创业之路是相当不现实的，”他说。

blocks.html_en: Steve, on the other hand, thinks he’s teaching his son a valuable lesson on self-reliance. “It’s not that I can’t afford to help him,” Steve told me. “I just have a real problem with handouts. And I want him to have the satisfaction of knowing he’s built something himself, with his own hard work. That’s how I was raised, too.

sentences.en: Steve, on the other hand, thinks he’s teaching his son a valuable lesson on self-reliance.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，史蒂夫认为他正在给儿子上一堂关于自力更生的宝贵课。

sentences.en: “It’s not that I can’t afford to help him,” Steve told me.
sentences.zh: “并不是我无力帮助他，”史蒂夫告诉我。

sentences.en: “I just have a real problem with handouts.
sentences.zh: “我只是对施舍有一个真正的问题。

sentences.en: And I want him to have the satisfaction of knowing he’s built something himself, with his own hard work.
sentences.zh: 我希望他能感到满足，因为他知道自己通过自己的努力创造了一些东西。

sentences.en: That’s how I was raised, too.
sentences.zh: 我也是这样长大的。

blocks.html_en: The virality of this piece prompted a discussion between Louise Perry and Rob Henderson: ‘The politics of the downwardly mobile class’.

sentences.en: The virality of this piece prompted a discussion between Louise Perry and Rob Henderson: ‘The politics of the downwardly mobile class’.
sentences.zh: 这篇文章的病毒式传播引发了路易丝·佩里和罗布·亨德森之间的讨论：“向下流动阶级的政治”。

blocks.html_en: Rob disagreed with the Cut article’s perspective:

sentences.en: Rob disagreed with the Cut article’s perspective:
sentences.zh: Rob 不同意 Cut 文章的观点：

blocks.html_en: The way that this article is written, it’s intended, I think, to convey that millennials are really struggling… but, generally, Millennials are doing just fine. And even the link to the US GAO that they cite says that Millennial households were more likely than other generations to be college educated… incomes have remained flat across the three generations… There was an article a couple of years ago from Jean Twenge in The Atlantic showing that Millennials are doing just as well as previous generations were.

sentences.en: The way that this article is written, it’s intended, I think, to convey that millennials are really struggling…
sentences.zh: 我认为，这篇文章的写作方式是为了传达千禧一代确实在挣扎......

sentences.en: but, generally, Millennials are doing just fine.
sentences.zh: 但总的来说，千禧一代的表现还不错。

sentences.en: And even the link to the US GAO that they cite says that Millennial households were more likely than other generations to be college educated…
sentences.zh: 甚至他们引用的美国政府问责局的链接也表明，千禧一代家庭比其他几代人更有可能接受大学教育……

sentences.en: incomes have remained flat across the three generations…
sentences.zh: 三代人的收入保持平稳……

sentences.en: There was an article a couple of years ago from Jean Twenge in The Atlantic showing that Millennials are doing just as well as previous generations were.
sentences.zh: 几年前，Jean Twenge 在《大西洋月刊》上发表了一篇文章，表明千禧一代的表现与前几代人一样好。

blocks.html_en: I disagree! I think that Millennials and Zoomers are not doing fine. In fact, I think there have been structural changes to our economy and society which clearly explain why these cohorts are failing to move through the five pillars of a stable middle-class existence: education, stable employment, marriage, homeownership, children.

sentences.en: I disagree!
sentences.zh: 我不同意！

sentences.en: I think that Millennials and Zoomers are not doing fine.
sentences.zh: 我认为千禧一代和变焦一代的表现并不好。

sentences.en: In fact, I think there have been structural changes to our economy and society which clearly explain why these cohorts are failing to move through the five pillars of a stable middle-class existence: education, stable employment, marriage, homeownership, children.
sentences.zh: 事实上，我认为我们的经济和社会发生了结构性变化，这清楚地解释了为什么这些群体未能实现稳定的中产阶级生存的五个支柱：教育、稳定就业、婚姻、拥有住房、孩子。

blocks.html_en: Some of these negative changes stand out in the normal datasets, while some do not, for reasons I will explain. In short, what has happened is that previous generations were able to take advantage of highly valuable and productive social capital as well as their exploding financial capital to move through each of these life stages in a fiscally efficient manner. (Don’t worry: I will define what I mean by social capital and back my argument with data).

sentences.en: Some of these negative changes stand out in the normal datasets, while some do not, for reasons I will explain.
sentences.zh: 其中一些负面变化在正常数据集中很突出，而另一些则不然，原因我将解释。

sentences.en: In short, what has happened is that previous generations were able to take advantage of highly valuable and productive social capital as well as their exploding financial capital to move through each of these life stages in a fiscally efficient manner.
sentences.zh: 简而言之，所发生的情况是，前几代人能够利用极具价值和生产性的社会资本以及爆炸性的金融资本，以财政有效的方式度过每个生命阶段。

sentences.en: (Don’t worry: I will define what I mean by social capital and back my argument with data).
sentences.zh: （别担心：我将定义社会资本的含义，并用数据支持我的论点）。

blocks.html_en: Our current young, lacking access to this social capital, must engage in enormous outlays of purely financial capital in order to achieve the same levels of stability and accomplishment — capital that they do not have. We see this clearly in the data on schooling, homeownership, and family formation.

sentences.en: Our current young, lacking access to this social capital, must engage in enormous outlays of purely financial capital in order to achieve the same levels of stability and accomplishment — capital that they do not have.
sentences.zh: 我们现在的年轻人缺乏获得这种社会资本的机会，必须投入大量的纯金融资本，才能达到同样水平的稳定性和成就——他们没有这种资本。

sentences.en: We see this clearly in the data on schooling, homeownership, and family formation.
sentences.zh: 我们从学校教育、住房拥有率和家庭组建的数据中清楚地看到了这一点。

blocks.html_en: All of this is hidden from the top-line metrics, usually in one of two ways:

sentences.en: All of this is hidden from the top-line metrics, usually in one of two ways:
sentences.zh: 所有这些都隐藏在顶线指标中，通常通过以下两种方式之一：

blocks.html_en: • Huge increases in the cost of essential aspects of family life (homeownership, education, etc.) being offset by huge decreases in the cost of non-essential goods (televisions, phones, etc.) making the young appear wealthy in the abstract but without them actually being able to afford anything meaningful;

sentences.en: Huge increases in the cost of essential aspects of family life (homeownership, education, etc.) being offset by huge decreases in the cost of non-essential goods (televisions, phones, etc.) making the young appear wealthy in the abstract but without them actually being able to afford anything meaningful;
sentences.zh: 家庭生活的基本方面（住房、教育等）成本的大幅增加被非必需品（电视、电话等）成本的大幅下降所抵消，这使得年轻人在抽象上看起来很富有，但实际上却买不起任何有意义的东西；

blocks.html_en: • By presenting false equivalencies which imply a level of choice that younger generations do not actually have. For example: an American family in 1975 could send their children to public school on the assumption that the vast majority of other children would belong to intact families, communities like their own, and would speak English as a first language. Now, realistically, many parents must turn to private schooling for the same reassurances.

sentences.en: By presenting false equivalencies which imply a level of choice that younger generations do not actually have.
sentences.zh: 通过提供虚假的等同性，暗示年轻一代实际上没有选择的水平。

sentences.en: For example: an American family in 1975 could send their children to public school on the assumption that the vast majority of other children would belong to intact families, communities like their own, and would speak English as a first language.
sentences.zh: 例如：1975 年的一个美国家庭可以将他们的孩子送到公立学校，前提是绝大多数其他孩子都属于完整的家庭、像他们自己的社区一样，并且会说英语作为第一语言。

sentences.en: Now, realistically, many parents must turn to private schooling for the same reassurances.
sentences.zh: 现在，实际上，许多家长必须转向私立学校以获得同样的保证。

blocks.html_en: On the surface it does seem as if the young are fine. Real median incomes are higher than they were for their parents at the same age, unemployment has spent most of the past decade at historic lows, and purchasing power has risen roughly 63 percent since 1973. Televisions, clothing, food, and air travel are cheaper than ever.

sentences.en: On the surface it does seem as if the young are fine.
sentences.zh: 从表面上看，年轻人确实很好。

sentences.en: Real median incomes are higher than they were for their parents at the same age, unemployment has spent most of the past decade at historic lows, and purchasing power has risen roughly 63 percent since 1973.
sentences.zh: 实际收入中位数高于同龄父辈，失业率在过去十年的大部分时间里都处于历史低点，购买力自 1973 年以来增长了约 63%。

sentences.en: Televisions, clothing, food, and air travel are cheaper than ever.
sentences.zh: 电视、衣服、食物和航空旅行比以往任何时候都便宜。

blocks.html_en: And yet this generation is also not marrying, not buying homes, not having children, and seem pretty miserable. I think we’re clearly in the midst of a tremendous measurement failure.

sentences.en: And yet this generation is also not marrying, not buying homes, not having children, and seem pretty miserable.
sentences.zh: 然而，这一代人也不结婚，不买房，不生孩子，看起来相当悲惨。

sentences.en: I think we’re clearly in the midst of a tremendous measurement failure.
sentences.zh: 我认为我们显然正处于巨大的测量失败之中。

blocks.html_en: My argument is that previous generations received an enormous stock of social capital: trusted neighbors, functional public schools, a productive courtship culture, predictable career arcs, and a public square in which children could roam and adults could be relied upon. That stock, once given for free, has now been substantially liquidated.

sentences.en: My argument is that previous generations received an enormous stock of social capital: trusted neighbors, functional public schools, a productive courtship culture, predictable career arcs, and a public square in which children could roam and adults could be relied upon.
sentences.zh: 我的观点是，前几代人获得了大量的社会资本：值得信赖的邻居、功能齐全的公立学校、富有成效的求爱文化、可预测的职业弧线，以及孩子们可以漫步、成年人可以信赖的公共广场。

sentences.en: That stock, once given for free, has now been substantially liquidated.
sentences.zh: 该股票曾经免费提供，现在已被大量清算。

blocks.html_en: Instead, the young must now buy back, item by item and at retail prices, what their grandparents received as a bounty of prior civilizational investments. The young must do so out of incomes that rose modestly while the prices of the essential elements of life rose radically. Price indexes measure the individual cost of discrete goods, but they are not intended to convey the total cost of personally repurchasing a destroyed commons.

sentences.en: Instead, the young must now buy back, item by item and at retail prices, what their grandparents received as a bounty of prior civilizational investments.
sentences.zh: 相反，年轻人现在必须以零售价一件一件地买回他们的祖父母作为先前文明投资的恩惠而获得的东西。

sentences.en: The young must do so out of incomes that rose modestly while the prices of the essential elements of life rose radically.
sentences.zh: 年轻人必须在收入小幅增长的情况下做到这一点，而生活必需品的价格却大幅上涨。

sentences.en: Price indexes measure the individual cost of discrete goods, but they are not intended to convey the total cost of personally repurchasing a destroyed commons.
sentences.zh: 价格指数衡量的是离散商品的单独成本，但它们并不是为了传达个人回购被毁坏的公共物品的总成本。

blocks.html_en: This type of failure is well understood as a threat in economics. The old joke is that when a man marries his cleaner, the GDP of both households collapses even while the actual labor being done remains the same and everyone is better off. In our case, we’re seeing the opposite: a thousand small divorces and social fragmentations which boost the appearance of GDP but leave everyone poorer in reality.

sentences.en: This type of failure is well understood as a threat in economics.
sentences.zh: 这种类型的失败被充分理解为经济学中的威胁。

sentences.en: The old joke is that when a man marries his cleaner, the GDP of both households collapses even while the actual labor being done remains the same and everyone is better off.
sentences.zh: 有一个老笑话是，当一个男人与他的清洁工结婚时，两个家庭的国内生产总值都会崩溃，尽管实际所做的劳动保持不变，每个人的生活都会变得更好。

sentences.en: In our case, we’re seeing the opposite: a thousand small divorces and social fragmentations which boost the appearance of GDP but leave everyone poorer in reality.
sentences.zh: 在我们的例子中，我们看到了相反的情况：上千起小离婚和社会分裂，这些都提高了国内生产总值的表象，但实际上却让每个人都变得更加贫穷。

blocks.html_en: Occasionally, researchers succeed in capturing and modelling these hidden transitions, and we get a glimpse into the deep faultlines under society — direct evidence that official measures miss what households actually feel. In 2024, for example, a compelling study by Lawrence Summers and colleagues showed that the gap between depressed consumer sentiment and cheerful official statistics closes once borrowing costs (excluded from the modern CPI but important for family finances) are counted as part of the cost of living. The consumers were right.

sentences.en: Occasionally, researchers succeed in capturing and modelling these hidden transitions, and we get a glimpse into the deep faultlines under society — direct evidence that official measures miss what households actually feel.
sentences.zh: 有时，研究人员成功地捕捉和模拟这些隐藏的转变，我们就能一睹社会深层的断层线——直接证据表明官方措施忽视了家庭的实际感受。

sentences.en: In 2024, for example, a compelling study by Lawrence Summers and colleagues showed that the gap between depressed consumer sentiment and cheerful official statistics closes once borrowing costs (excluded from the modern CPI but important for family finances) are counted as part of the cost of living.
sentences.zh: 例如，劳伦斯·萨默斯（Lawrence Summers）及其同事在 2024 年进行的一项引人注目的研究表明，一旦借贷成本（不包括在现代 CPI 中，但对家庭财务很重要）被计入生活成本的一部分，低迷的消费者信心和乐观的官方统计数据之间的差距就会缩小。

sentences.en: The consumers were right.
sentences.zh: 消费者是对的。

blocks.html_en: What actually got cheaper over the past fifty years? Electronics, entertainment, fast fashion, processed food, toys, screens of every kind. And what got more expensive, usually by many multiples? Housing, education, childcare, healthcare, insurance.

sentences.en: What actually got cheaper over the past fifty years?
sentences.zh: 在过去的五十年里，什么东西实际上变得更便宜了？

sentences.en: Electronics, entertainment, fast fashion, processed food, toys, screens of every kind.
sentences.zh: 电子、娱乐、快时尚、加工食品、玩具、各种屏幕。

sentences.en: And what got more expensive, usually by many multiples?
sentences.zh: 什么东西变得更贵，通常是好几倍？

sentences.en: Housing, education, childcare, healthcare, insurance.
sentences.zh: 住房、教育、儿童保育、医疗保健、保险。

blocks.html_en: Absurdly, both of these movements register in the statistics as progress: one shows up as asset appreciation and the other as consumer surplus. But the lived reality for families feels like a pincer.

sentences.en: Absurdly, both of these movements register in the statistics as progress: one shows up as asset appreciation and the other as consumer surplus.
sentences.zh: 荒谬的是，这两种运动在统计数据中都被记录为进步：一种表现为资产增值，另一种表现为消费者剩余。

sentences.en: But the lived reality for families feels like a pincer.
sentences.zh: 但对于家庭来说，现实生活就像一把钳子。

blocks.html_en: Housing is the least ambiguous case. At age 30, 55 percent of the Silent Generation owned homes. For Boomers the figure was 48 percent, for Gen X 42 percent, and for Millennials 33 percent. The national ratio of median home price to median household income, which stood around 3.2 through the 1990s, reached 5.0 in 2024, nearly matching its all-time high. The median age of the first-time buyer, 29 in the early 1980s, hit a record 40 in 2025. Whatever supposed wage gains the young have enjoyed, housing claws most of them back before they can be converted into anything durable.

sentences.en: Housing is the least ambiguous case.
sentences.zh: 住房是最明确的情况。

sentences.en: At age 30, 55 percent of the Silent Generation owned homes.
sentences.zh: 30 岁时，55% 的沉默一代拥有住房。

sentences.en: For Boomers the figure was 48 percent, for Gen X 42 percent, and for Millennials 33 percent.
sentences.zh: 婴儿潮一代的这一数字为 48%，X 一代为 42%，千禧一代为 33%。

sentences.en: The national ratio of median home price to median household income, which stood around 3.2 through the 1990s, reached 5.0 in 2024, nearly matching its all-time high.
sentences.zh: 全国房价中位数与家庭收入中位数的比率在 1990 年代约为 3.2，到 2024 年达到 5.0，几乎达到历史最高水平。

sentences.en: The median age of the first-time buyer, 29 in the early 1980s, hit a record 40 in 2025.
sentences.zh: 首次购房者的平均年龄在 20 世纪 80 年代初为 29 岁，到 2025 年将达到创纪录的 40 岁。

sentences.en: Whatever supposed wage gains the young have enjoyed, housing claws most of them back before they can be converted into anything durable.
sentences.zh: 无论年轻人享受了所谓的工资增长，住房在他们被转化为任何耐用的东西之前就将他们中的大多数人夺走了。

blocks.html_en: Significant changes in rent have made this worse. The standard escape from renting is saving for a down payment, but soaring rent has made saving near impossible. Roughly half of all renter households now spend more than 30 percent of their income on rent and utilities, the federal threshold for being “cost-burdened,” and more than a quarter spend over half. Layer exploding student debt on top — of which more below — and you get a class of people in permanent limbo.

sentences.en: Significant changes in rent have made this worse.
sentences.zh: 租金的重大变化使情况变得更糟。

sentences.en: The standard escape from renting is saving for a down payment, but soaring rent has made saving near impossible.
sentences.zh: 逃避租房的标准做法是存钱以支付首付，但飙升的租金使存钱几乎变得不可能。

sentences.en: Roughly half of all renter households now spend more than 30 percent of their income on rent and utilities, the federal threshold for being “cost-burdened,” and more than a quarter spend over half.
sentences.zh: 目前，大约一半的租房家庭将其收入的 30% 以上用于租金和水电费，这是联邦“成本负担”的门槛，超过四分之一的家庭的支出超过了一半。

sentences.en: Layer exploding student debt on top — of which more below — and you get a class of people in permanent limbo.
sentences.zh: 将爆炸式增长的学生债务放在上面——下面还有更多——你会得到一群永远处于困境的人。

blocks.html_en: One rebuttal you hear to this predicament concerns square footage. The line is that due to consumer preference, houses today are larger than the bungalows of 1955, so price per square foot has risen less than price per house; the young, the argument goes, are demanding more luxury and are pricing themselves out of the market.

sentences.en: One rebuttal you hear to this predicament concerns square footage.
sentences.zh: 您听到的针对这一困境的一个反驳与平方英尺有关。

sentences.en: The line is that due to consumer preference, houses today are larger than the bungalows of 1955, so price per square foot has risen less than price per house; the young, the argument goes, are demanding more luxury and are pricing themselves out of the market.
sentences.zh: 关键是，由于消费者的偏好，今天的房屋比 1955 年的平房更大，因此每平方英尺的价格涨幅低于每栋房屋的价格；有人认为，年轻人需要更多的奢侈品，并且正在将自己挤出市场。

blocks.html_en: This is unconvincing on two counts. First, the market barely offers the smaller option: zoning regimes across the country make it easier to build large houses than small ones, and buyers who would prefer modest homes in decent areas frequently cannot find them. Scott Alexander described living alone in a three-bedroom house in Michigan because no acceptable one-bedroom existed near his workplace and the apartments were (his words!) ‘loud and crime-y’.

sentences.en: This is unconvincing on two counts.
sentences.zh: 这在两个方面是不令人信服的。

sentences.en: First, the market barely offers the smaller option: zoning regimes across the country make it easier to build large houses than small ones, and buyers who would prefer modest homes in decent areas frequently cannot find them.
sentences.zh: 首先，市场几乎没有提供较小的选择：全国各地的分区制度使得建造大房子比建造小房子更容易，而那些喜欢在体面地区建造简朴房屋的买家往往找不到它们。

sentences.en: Scott Alexander described living alone in a three-bedroom house in Michigan because no acceptable one-bedroom existed near his workplace and the apartments were (his words!)
sentences.zh: 斯科特·亚历山大 (Scott Alexander) 描述了他独自住在密歇根州一栋三居室的房子里的情况，因为他的工作场所附近没有可接受的一居室，而且这些公寓（他的话！）

sentences.en: ‘loud and crime-y’.
sentences.zh: “大声且犯罪”。

blocks.html_en: I think this latter point is a generalizable pattern. As Mr. Alexander discovered, the added, expensive square footage — like private school — is a hedge against public dysfunction and is another example of a replacement purchase. The over-priced neighborhood is perversely desirable when pricing is the only legal means of discrimination in an increasingly dysfunctional society.

sentences.en: I think this latter point is a generalizable pattern.
sentences.zh: 我认为后一点是一个普遍的模式。

sentences.en: As Mr. Alexander discovered, the added, expensive square footage — like private school — is a hedge against public dysfunction and is another example of a replacement purchase.
sentences.zh: 正如亚历山大先生发现的那样，增加的昂贵面积（如私立学校）是对公共功能障碍的对冲，也是替代购买的另一个例子。

sentences.en: The over-priced neighborhood is perversely desirable when pricing is the only legal means of discrimination in an increasingly dysfunctional society.
sentences.zh: 当定价成为日益功能失调的社会中唯一合法的歧视手段时，价格过高的社区反而令人向往。

blocks.html_en:
Free childhoods disappeared alongside the loss of something harder to measure: the trust that makes a child outdoors seem normal rather than negligent and the local conditions that once made such trust reasonable. These conditions - like many other kinds of social integrity such as intact two-parent homes - have become aggressively sorted by class…

The childhood depicted in nostalgic media rested on a dense web of adults who knew each other, shared a rough moral sense, and could be relied upon to mind each other’s children. That web is now a feature of particular places (often expensive places) rather than a general inheritance, and discerning which places have kept it is of key importance for families hoping to raise agentic children with deep networks of trusted friends.

Perversely, this turns ‘overpriced homes’ into a feature, rather than a bug. When families have no organic access to likeminded, competent, stable, trustworthy families, they use one of the only mechanisms left which forces some kind of homogeneity: money. People’s revealed preference is to live in arbitrarily expensive neighborhoods because the cost is a moat against endemic dysfunction. Or, as one X user put it, families must escape the tyranny of the bottom quintile which has been allowed to run rampant.
— Childhood freedom is a now luxury good

sentences.zh: 自由的童年消失了，同时也失去了一些更难以衡量的东西：让孩子在户外看起来很正常而不是疏忽的信任，以及曾经使这种信任合理的当地条件。

sentences.zh: 这些条件 - 就像许多其他类型的社会诚信，例如完整的双亲家庭 - 已被积极地按阶级分类......

sentences.zh: 怀旧媒体所描绘的童年建立在一张由成年人组成的密集网络上，这些成年人彼此认识，有共同的道德观念，并且可以信赖地照顾彼此的孩子。

sentences.zh: 当家庭无法有机地接触到志趣相投、有能力、稳定、值得信赖的家庭时，他们就会使用唯一剩下的机制之一来迫使某种同质化：金钱。

sentences.zh: 人们表现出的偏好是居住在任意昂贵的社区，因为成本是防止地方性功能障碍的护城河。

blocks.html_en: The socializing that happened in parks, neighborhoods, boy scouts, etc. now must happen in the private domain. A bigger house, which includes a large playroom and yard, is the private replacement for a public square that no longer functions and is thus necessary infrastructure as much as luxury.

sentences.en: The socializing that happened in parks, neighborhoods, boy scouts, etc. now must happen in the private domain.
sentences.zh: 发生在公园、社区、童子军等地方的社交活动现在必须发生在私人领域。

sentences.en: A bigger house, which includes a large playroom and yard, is the private replacement for a public square that no longer functions and is thus necessary infrastructure as much as luxury.
sentences.zh: 更大的房子，包括一个大的游戏室和庭院，是不再发挥作用的公共广场的私人替代品，因此是必要的基础设施和奢侈品。

blocks.html_en: An owned home is the material base of psychological stability: it fixes housing costs against inflation, converts rent into equity, roots a family in a particular place among particular neighbors, and provides the security without which people demonstrably hesitate to marry and reproduce.

sentences.en: An owned home is the material base of psychological stability: it fixes housing costs against inflation, converts rent into equity, roots a family in a particular place among particular neighbors, and provides the security without which people demonstrably hesitate to marry and reproduce.
sentences.zh: 拥有的住房是心理稳定的物质基础：它可以调节住房成本以应对通货膨胀，将租金转化为股本，使家庭在特定邻居中的特定地点扎根，并提供安全感，没有这种安全感，人们显然会犹豫是否结婚和生育。

blocks.html_en: Robert Sampson’s research on Chicago found that the collective efficacy which keeps neighborhoods safe — mutual trust plus the willingness of residents to intervene for the common good — is strongest where residents own their homes and stay put. Ownership is both a private good and the raw material of the social capital whose destruction started this spiral. A generation locked out of ownership is locked out of both.

sentences.en: Robert Sampson’s research on Chicago found that the collective efficacy which keeps neighborhoods safe — mutual trust plus the willingness of residents to intervene for the common good — is strongest where residents own their homes and stay put.
sentences.zh: 罗伯特·桑普森对芝加哥的研究发现，在居民拥有自己的房屋并留在原地的情况下，维护社区安全的集体效能——相互信任加上居民为共同利益进行干预的意愿——最为强烈。

sentences.en: Ownership is both a private good and the raw material of the social capital whose destruction started this spiral.
sentences.zh: 所有权既是私人物品，也是社会资本的原材料，社会资本的破坏引发了这种螺旋式上升。

sentences.en: A generation locked out of ownership is locked out of both.
sentences.zh: 被排除在所有权之外的一代人也被排除在两者之外。

blocks.html_en: Marriage follows the same pattern. Women now substantially outnumber men on university campuses and outpace them in degrees earned, yet the preference for husbands who match or exceed a wife’s income and education has not correspondingly relaxed. The result is a radical market mismatch.

sentences.en: Marriage follows the same pattern.
sentences.zh: 婚姻也遵循同样的模式。

sentences.en: Women now substantially outnumber men on university campuses and outpace them in degrees earned, yet the preference for husbands who match or exceed a wife’s income and education has not correspondingly relaxed.
sentences.zh: 现在，大学校园里的女性人数大大超过男性，获得的学位也超过男性，但对收入和教育程度与妻子相当或超过妻子的丈夫的偏好并没有相应放松。

sentences.en: The result is a radical market mismatch.
sentences.zh: 结果是市场彻底错配。

blocks.html_en: Daniel Lichter, Joseph Price, and Jeffrey Swigert constructed statistical profiles of the husbands that today’s unmarried women would marry, based on the actual husbands of demographically comparable married women. These “synthetic husbands” have incomes 58 percent higher than the real unmarried men available; they are 30 percent more likely to be employed and 19 percent more likely to hold a college degree. The men whom women are prepared to marry bear little resemblance to their struggling real-world counterparts. The few who shape up to the fantasy are snapped up quickly.

sentences.en: Daniel Lichter, Joseph Price, and Jeffrey Swigert constructed statistical profiles of the husbands that today’s unmarried women would marry, based on the actual husbands of demographically comparable married women.
sentences.zh: 丹尼尔·利希特 (Daniel Lichter)、约瑟夫·普莱斯 (Joseph Price) 和杰弗里·斯威格特 (Jeffrey Swigert) 根据人口统计上可比的已婚女性的实际丈夫，构建了当今未婚女性将结婚的丈夫的统计资料。

sentences.en: These “synthetic husbands” have incomes 58 percent higher than the real unmarried men available; they are 30 percent more likely to be employed and 19 percent more likely to hold a college degree.
sentences.zh: 这些“假丈夫”的收入比真正的未婚男性高出58%；他们就业的可能性高出 30%，拥有大学学位的可能性高出 19%。

sentences.en: The men whom women are prepared to marry bear little resemblance to their struggling real-world counterparts.
sentences.zh: 女性准备结婚的男性与现实世界中苦苦挣扎的男性几乎没有什么相似之处。

sentences.en: The few who shape up to the fantasy are snapped up quickly.
sentences.zh: 少数能够实现这种幻想的人很快就会被抢购一空。

blocks.html_en: For a regular man, this implies that becoming marriageable now requires clearing exceptional bars: a degree (with the debt that comes attached) and an income well above the male median (also — 6ft, muscular physique, etc. etc.). Likewise social media has raised the expectations against which men are measured to make-believe levels. A working man in 1965 was marriageable by default in a way that his grandson is not. Again, all of this leverages tremendous economic pressure on men to overperform.

sentences.en: For a regular man, this implies that becoming marriageable now requires clearing exceptional bars: a degree (with the debt that comes attached) and an income well above the male median (also — 6ft, muscular physique, etc. etc.).
sentences.zh: 对于一个普通男人来说，这意味着现在要结婚需要满足特殊的条件：学位（附带债务）和收入远高于男性中位数（还有——6英尺，肌肉发达，等等）。

sentences.en: Likewise social media has raised the expectations against which men are measured to make-believe levels.
sentences.zh: 同样，社交媒体也将衡量男性的期望提高到了虚构的水平。

sentences.en: A working man in 1965 was marriageable by default in a way that his grandson is not.
sentences.zh: 1965 年，一名工人默认可以结婚，但他的孙子则不然。

sentences.en: Again, all of this leverages tremendous economic pressure on men to overperform.
sentences.zh: 同样，所有这一切都给男性带来了巨大的经济压力，导致他们表现出色。

blocks.html_en: Supposing marriage and family formation is achieved, new parents discover that the activities formerly provided by ‘free-range childhoods’ must now be bought as expensive private services. Supervision that neighbors and older siblings once provided becomes daycare and after-school programs.

sentences.en: Supposing marriage and family formation is achieved, new parents discover that the activities formerly provided by ‘free-range childhoods’ must now be bought as expensive private services.
sentences.zh: 假设婚姻和家庭得以建立，新父母发现以前由“自由放养的童年”提供的活动现在必须作为昂贵的私人服务购买。

sentences.en: Supervision that neighbors and older siblings once provided becomes daycare and after-school programs.
sentences.zh: 邻居和年长的兄弟姐妹曾经提供的监督变成了日托和课后计划。

blocks.html_en: The average annual cost of childcare reached $13,128 in 2024, a 29 percent rise in four years; infant care now exceeds in-state college tuition in most of the US. All of this buys back, imperfectly and at great cost, what the 1960s family got free. “Intensive parenting,” routinely diagnosed as a cultural fashion, is a rational insurance response to a collapsed commons.

sentences.en: The average annual cost of childcare reached $13,128 in 2024, a 29 percent rise in four years; infant care now exceeds in-state college tuition in most of the US.
sentences.zh: 2024 年，儿童保育的年均费用达到 13,128 美元，四年内增长 29%；在美国大部分地区，婴儿护理费用现已超过州内大学学费。

sentences.en: All of this buys back, imperfectly and at great cost, what the 1960s family got free.
sentences.zh: 所有这一切都以不完美且付出巨大代价的方式买回了 20 世纪 60 年代家庭免费获得的东西。

sentences.en: “Intensive parenting,” routinely diagnosed as a cultural fashion, is a rational insurance response to a collapsed commons.
sentences.zh: “强化养育”通常被诊断为一种文化时尚，是对崩溃的公地的理性保险反应。

blocks.html_en: The transition to the economic default of the two-earner household intensifies these pressures. A household with a parent at home produces an enormous stream of untaxed, uncounted output: childcare, cooking and cleaning, budget management, and the daily maintenance of relations with other families (ie. social capital).

sentences.en: The transition to the economic default of the two-earner household intensifies these pressures.
sentences.zh: 双职工家庭向经济违约的转变加剧了这些压力。

sentences.en: A household with a parent at home produces an enormous stream of untaxed, uncounted output: childcare, cooking and cleaning, budget management, and the daily maintenance of relations with other families (ie.
sentences.zh: 有父母在家的家庭会产生大量未纳税、无法计算的产出：儿童保育、烹饪和清洁、预算管理以及与其他家庭关系的日常维护（即家庭关系）。

sentences.en: social capital).
sentences.zh: 社会资本）。

blocks.html_en: Move the at-home spouse into the labor market and every service she provided must be repurchased from the latest private equity roll-up (nursery, takeaway, cleaner, security system…). GDP records each purchase as growth. A family can thus be measured as substantially richer while consuming the same services — only now they spend less time together.

sentences.en: Move the at-home spouse into the labor market and every service she provided must be repurchased from the latest private equity roll-up (nursery, takeaway, cleaner, security system…).
sentences.zh: 将在家的配偶转移到劳动力市场，她提供的每项服务都必须从最新的私募股权投资中回购（托儿所、外卖、清洁工、安全系统……）。

sentences.en: GDP records each purchase as growth.
sentences.zh: GDP 将每次购买记录为增长。

sentences.en: A family can thus be measured as substantially richer while consuming the same services — only now they spend less time together.
sentences.zh: 因此，在消费相同服务的情况下，一个家庭可以被衡量为更加富裕——只是现在他们在一起的时间更少了。

blocks.html_en: Education is the same. The credential became increasingly mandatory and increasingly ruinous. This is where Rob Henderson’s analysis really misses the mark: if a hugely increased percentage of young people are going to college — and thus losing years of their lives and accruing record debt — without substantially increased earnings, that bodes very badly for the young indeed.

sentences.en: Education is the same.
sentences.zh: 教育也是一样。

sentences.en: The credential became increasingly mandatory and increasingly ruinous.
sentences.zh: 证书变得越来越强制性，也越来越具有破坏性。

sentences.en: This is where Rob Henderson’s analysis really misses the mark: if a hugely increased percentage of young people are going to college — and thus losing years of their lives and accruing record debt — without substantially increased earnings, that bodes very badly for the young indeed.
sentences.zh: 这就是罗布·亨德森的分析确实没有切中要害的地方：如果上大学的年轻人比例大幅增加——从而损失了生命中的岁月并积累了创纪录的债务——而收入却没有大幅增加，这对年轻人来说确实是一个非常糟糕的兆头。

blocks.html_en: In a service economy with proliferating occupational licensing and degree screening, the bachelor’s degree is now required in almost all dynamic sectors. I used to work for a company whose founder made a lot of noise about not requiring degrees! In practice, based on my experience both being interviewed and later interviewing and hiring others, the company absolutely did require degrees (in fact, it required the very best ones and prestigious graduate studies were desirable).

sentences.en: In a service economy with proliferating occupational licensing and degree screening, the bachelor’s degree is now required in almost all dynamic sectors.
sentences.zh: 在职业许可和学位筛选激增的服务经济中，现在几乎所有充满活力的行业都需要学士学位。

sentences.en: I used to work for a company whose founder made a lot of noise about not requiring degrees!
sentences.zh: 我曾经在一家公司工作，该公司的创始人大声疾呼不需要学位！

sentences.en: In practice, based on my experience both being interviewed and later interviewing and hiring others, the company absolutely did require degrees (in fact, it required the very best ones and prestigious graduate studies were desirable).
sentences.zh: 在实践中，根据我面试以及后来面试和雇用其他人的经验，该公司绝对需要学位（事实上，它需要最好的学位，并且著名的研究生学习是可取的）。

blocks.html_en: Real tuition and fees have roughly tripled at public universities since 1990 and risen about fivefold since 1970; average debt at graduation has risen 41 percent in real terms just since 2007, and total student debt now approaches $1.8 trillion. For a large fraction of borrowers the wage premium never offsets the balance, and the debt functions purely as a tax on family formation: it raises the savings threshold for a down payment, delays marriage, and delays or forecloses children.

sentences.en: Real tuition and fees have roughly tripled at public universities since 1990 and risen about fivefold since 1970; average debt at graduation has risen 41 percent in real terms just since 2007, and total student debt now approaches $1.8 trillion.
sentences.zh: 自 1990 年以来，公立大学的实际学杂费大约增加了两倍，自 1970 年以来增加了约五倍；自 2007 年以来，毕业时的平均债务实际增长了 41%，学生债务总额现已接近 1.8 万亿美元。

sentences.en: For a large fraction of borrowers the wage premium never offsets the balance, and the debt functions purely as a tax on family formation: it raises the savings threshold for a down payment, delays marriage, and delays or forecloses children.
sentences.zh: 对于很大一部分借款人来说，工资溢价永远不会抵消余额，债务的作用纯粹是对家庭形成的征税：它提高了首付的储蓄门槛，推迟结婚，推迟或取消孩子的赎回权。

blocks.html_en: And beneath higher education sits the same repurchasing dynamic at the school level. The public school was among the most valuable components of the inherited commons: perhaps not perfect, but usually a free, orderly, locally trusted institution that a family got with its address. Where it has decayed into disorder — and urban district performance and safety data leave little doubt that in many places it has — families face tough choices.

sentences.en: And beneath higher education sits the same repurchasing dynamic at the school level.
sentences.zh: 在高等教育之下，学校层面也存在同样的回购动力。

sentences.en: The public school was among the most valuable components of the inherited commons: perhaps not perfect, but usually a free, orderly, locally trusted institution that a family got with its address.
sentences.zh: 公立学校是继承公地中最有价值的组成部分之一：也许并不完美，但通常是一个自由、有序、当地值得信赖的机构，一个家庭可以通过其地址获得。

sentences.en: Where it has decayed into disorder — and urban district performance and safety data leave little doubt that in many places it has — families face tough choices.
sentences.zh: 在它已经陷入混乱的地方——市区的表现和安全数据毫无疑问地表明，在许多地方它已经陷入混乱——家庭面临着艰难的选择。

blocks.html_en: The cheap and effective Catholic school system of old has largely collapsed due to the dearth of nuns. And so the options are pay private tuition (which has risen about 158 percent in real terms since 1973), pay the housing premium for a catchment with a functional school, or pay in parental labor through homeschooling and forfeit the second income on which the broader economy is calibrated. The “good school district” premium is a particularly pure example of social capital being financialized and resold as real estate.

sentences.en: The cheap and effective Catholic school system of old has largely collapsed due to the dearth of nuns.
sentences.zh: 由于修女的缺乏，旧时廉价而有效的天主教学校系统已基本崩溃。

sentences.en: And so the options are pay private tuition (which has risen about 158 percent in real terms since 1973), pay the housing premium for a catchment with a functional school, or pay in parental labor through homeschooling and forfeit the second income on which the broader economy is calibrated.

The Cut 最近发表的一篇文章引起了广泛关注：“当我们在挣扎时，很难看到我的父母生活得如此奢侈”。 该作品包括千年苦难的小插曲：

...他的父亲 Steve 大约五年前将他的公司卖给了一家私募股权公司，这使他能够在 60 多岁的时候舒适地退休...两年前，Joe 向他的父亲申请贷款。 他想开办草坪护理业务，需要资金购买商业级设备和拖车，至少 15,000 美元。 他向史蒂夫提出了他的商业计划，其中包括还款时间表。 尽管如此，史蒂夫还是说“不”。 乔还没有开始创业。 他在一家物业管理公司工作，并兼职做园林绿化工作以赚取额外收入。 但他自己有两个年幼的孩子，很难挽救。 “如果没有一些初步的帮助，我想要走上创业之路是相当不现实的，”他说。 另一方面，史蒂夫认为他正在给儿子上一堂关于自力更生的宝贵课。 “并不是我无力帮助他，”史蒂夫告诉我。 “我只是对施舍有一个真正的问题。 我希望他能感到满足，因为他知道自己通过自己的努力创造了一些东西。 我也是这样长大的。

这篇文章的病毒式传播引发了路易丝·佩里和罗布·亨德森之间的讨论：“向下流动阶级的政治”。

Rob 不同意 Cut 文章的观点：

我认为，这篇文章的写作方式是为了传达千禧一代确实在挣扎...... 但总的来说，千禧一代的表现还不错。 甚至他们引用的美国政府问责局的链接也表明，千禧一代家庭比其他几代人更有可能接受大学教育…… 三代人的收入保持平稳…… 几年前，Jean Twenge 在《大西洋月刊》上发表了一篇文章，表明千禧一代的表现与前几代人一样好。

我不同意！ 我认为千禧一代和变焦一代的表现并不好。 事实上，我认为我们的经济和社会发生了结构性变化，这清楚地解释了为什么这些群体未能实现稳定的中产阶级生存的五个支柱：教育、稳定就业、婚姻、拥有住房、孩子。

其中一些负面变化在正常数据集中很突出，而另一些则不然，原因我将解释。 简而言之，所发生的情况是，前几代人能够利用极具价值和生产性的社会资本以及爆炸性的金融资本，以财政有效的方式度过每个生命阶段。 （别担心：我将定义社会资本的含义，并用数据支持我的论点）。

我们现在的年轻人缺乏获得这种社会资本的机会，必须投入大量的纯金融资本，才能达到同样水平的稳定性和成就——他们没有这种资本。 我们从学校教育、住房拥有率和家庭组建的数据中清楚地看到了这一点。

所有这些都隐藏在顶线指标中，通常通过以下两种方式之一：

  1. 家庭生活的基本方面（住房、教育等）成本的大幅增加被非必需品（电视、电话等）成本的大幅下降所抵消，这使得年轻人在抽象上看起来很富有，但实际上却买不起任何有意义的东西；
  2. 通过提供虚假的等同性，暗示年轻一代实际上没有选择的水平。 例如：1975 年的一个美国家庭可以将他们的孩子送到公立学校，前提是绝大多数其他孩子都属于完整的家庭、像他们自己的社区一样，并且会说英语作为第一语言。 现在，实际上，许多家长必须转向私立学校以获得同样的保证。

从表面上看，年轻人确实很好。 实际收入中位数高于同龄父辈，失业率在过去十年的大部分时间里都处于历史低点，购买力自 1973 年以来增长了约 63%。 电视、衣服、食物和航空旅行比以往任何时候都便宜。

然而，这一代人也不结婚，不买房，不生孩子，看起来相当悲惨。 我认为我们显然正处于巨大的测量失败之中。

我的观点是，前几代人获得了大量的社会资本：值得信赖的邻居、功能齐全的公立学校、富有成效的求爱文化、可预测的职业弧线，以及孩子们可以漫步、成年人可以信赖的公共广场。 该股票曾经免费提供，现在已被大量清算。

相反，年轻人现在必须以零售价一件一件地买回他们的祖父母作为先前文明投资的恩惠而获得的东西。 年轻人必须在收入小幅增长的情况下做到这一点，而生活必需品的价格却大幅上涨。 价格指数衡量的是离散商品的单独成本，但它们并不是为了传达个人回购被毁坏的公共物品的总成本。

这种类型的失败被充分理解为经济学中的威胁。 有一个老笑话是，当一个男人与他的清洁工结婚时，两个家庭的国内生产总值都会崩溃，尽管实际所做的劳动保持不变，每个人的生活都会变得更好。 在我们的例子中，我们看到了相反的情况：上千起小离婚和社会分裂，这些都提高了国内生产总值的表象，但实际上却让每个人都变得更加贫穷。

有时，研究人员成功地捕捉和模拟这些隐藏的转变，我们就能一睹社会深层的断层线——直接证据表明官方措施忽视了家庭的实际感受。 例如，劳伦斯·萨默斯（Lawrence Summers）及其同事在 2024 年进行的一项引人注目的研究表明，一旦借贷成本（不包括在现代 CPI 中，但对家庭财务很重要）被计入生活成本的一部分，低迷的消费者信心和乐观的官方统计数据之间的差距就会缩小。 消费者是对的。

在过去的五十年里，什么东西实际上变得更便宜了？ 电子、娱乐、快时尚、加工食品、玩具、各种屏幕。 什么东西变得更贵，通常是好几倍？ 住房、教育、儿童保育、医疗保健、保险。

荒谬的是，这两种运动在统计数据中都被记录为进步：一种表现为资产增值，另一种表现为消费者剩余。 但对于家庭来说，现实生活就像一把钳子。

住房是最明确的情况。 30 岁时，55% 的沉默一代拥有住房。 婴儿潮一代的这一数字为 48%，X 一代为 42%，千禧一代为 33%。 全国房价中位数与家庭收入中位数的比率在 1990 年代约为 3.2，到 2024 年达到 5.0，几乎达到历史最高水平。 首次购房者的平均年龄在 20 世纪 80 年代初为 29 岁，到 2025 年将达到创纪录的 40 岁。 无论年轻人享受了所谓的工资增长，住房在他们被转化为任何耐用的东西之前就将他们中的大多数人夺走了。

租金的重大变化使情况变得更糟。 逃避租房的标准做法是存钱以支付首付，但飙升的租金使存钱几乎变得不可能。 目前，大约一半的租房家庭将其收入的 30% 以上用于租金和水电费，这是联邦“成本负担”的门槛，超过四分之一的家庭的支出超过了一半。 将爆炸式增长的学生债务放在上面——下面还有更多——你会得到一群永远处于困境的人。

您听到的针对这一困境的一个反驳与平方英尺有关。 关键是，由于消费者的偏好，今天的房屋比 1955 年的平房更大，因此每平方英尺的价格涨幅低于每栋房屋的价格；有人认为，年轻人需要更多的奢侈品，并且正在将自己挤出市场。

这在两个方面是不令人信服的。 首先，市场几乎没有提供较小的选择：全国各地的分区制度使得建造大房子比建造小房子更容易，而那些喜欢在体面地区建造简朴房屋的买家往往找不到它们。 斯科特·亚历山大 (Scott Alexander) 描述了他独自住在密歇根州一栋三居室的房子里的情况，因为他的工作场所附近没有可接受的一居室，而且这些公寓（他的话！） “大声且犯罪”。

我认为后一点是一个普遍的模式。 正如亚历山大先生发现的那样，增加的昂贵面积（如私立学校）是对公共功能障碍的对冲，也是替代购买的另一个例子。 当定价成为日益功能失调的社会中唯一合法的歧视手段时，价格过高的社区反而令人向往。

自由的童年消失了，同时也失去了一些更难以衡量的东西：让孩子在户外看起来很正常而不是疏忽的信任，以及曾经使这种信任合理的当地条件。 这些条件 - 就像许多其他类型的社会诚信，例如完整的双亲家庭 - 已被积极地按阶级分类...... 怀旧媒体所描绘的童年建立在一张由成年人组成的密集网络上，这些成年人彼此认识，有共同的道德观念，并且可以信赖地照顾彼此的孩子。 现在，这个网络是特定地方（通常是昂贵的地方）的一个特征，而不是普遍的遗产，而辨别哪些地方保留了它对于那些希望通过值得信赖的朋友的深厚网络来抚养孩子的家庭来说至关重要。 反常的是，这将“价格过高的房屋”变成了一个功能，而不是一个错误。 当家庭无法有机地接触到志趣相投、有能力、稳定、值得信赖的家庭时，他们就会使用唯一剩下的机制之一来迫使某种同质化：金钱。 人们表现出的偏好是居住在任意昂贵的社区，因为成本是防止地方性功能障碍的护城河。 或者，正如一位 X 用户所说，家庭必须摆脱底层五分之一的暴政，这种暴政已经被允许猖獗。 — 童年的自由如今已成为奢侈品

发生在公园、社区、童子军等地方的社交活动现在必须发生在私人领域。 更大的房子，包括一个大的游戏室和庭院，是不再发挥作用的公共广场的私人替代品，因此是必要的基础设施和奢侈品。

拥有的住房是心理稳定的物质基础：它可以调节住房成本以应对通货膨胀，将租金转化为股本，使家庭在特定邻居中的特定地点扎根，并提供安全感，没有这种安全感，人们显然会犹豫是否结婚和生育。

罗伯特·桑普森对芝加哥的研究发现，在居民拥有自己的房屋并留在原地的情况下，维护社区安全的集体效能——相互信任加上居民为共同利益进行干预的意愿——最为强烈。 所有权既是私人物品，也是社会资本的原材料，社会资本的破坏引发了这种螺旋式上升。 被排除在所有权之外的一代人也被排除在两者之外。

婚姻也遵循同样的模式。 现在，大学校园里的女性人数大大超过男性，获得的学位也超过男性，但对收入和教育程度与妻子相当或超过妻子的丈夫的偏好并没有相应放松。 结果是市场彻底错配。

丹尼尔·利希特 (Daniel Lichter)、约瑟夫·普莱斯 (Joseph Price) 和杰弗里·斯威格特 (Jeffrey Swigert) 根据人口统计上可比的已婚女性的实际丈夫，构建了当今未婚女性将结婚的丈夫的统计资料。 这些“假丈夫”的收入比真正的未婚男性高出58%；他们就业的可能性高出 30%，拥有大学学位的可能性高出 19%。 女性准备结婚的男性与现实世界中苦苦挣扎的男性几乎没有什么相似之处。 少数能够实现这种幻想的人很快就会被抢购一空。

对于一个普通男人来说，这意味着现在要结婚需要满足特殊的条件：学位（附带债务）和收入远高于男性中位数（还有——6英尺，肌肉发达，等等）。 同样，社交媒体也将衡量男性的期望提高到了虚构的水平。 1965 年，一名工人默认可以结婚，但他的孙子则不然。 同样，所有这一切都给男性带来了巨大的经济压力，导致他们表现出色。

假设婚姻和家庭得以建立，新父母发现以前由“自由放养的童年”提供的活动现在必须作为昂贵的私人服务购买。 邻居和年长的兄弟姐妹曾经提供的监督变成了日托和课后计划。

2024 年，儿童保育的年均费用达到 13,128 美元，四年内增长 29%；在美国大部分地区，婴儿护理费用现已超过州内大学学费。 所有这一切都以不完美且付出巨大代价的方式买回了 20 世纪 60 年代家庭免费获得的东西。 “强化养育”通常被诊断为一种文化时尚，是对崩溃的公地的理性保险反应。

双职工家庭向经济违约的转变加剧了这些压力。 有父母在家的家庭会产生大量未纳税、无法计算的产出：儿童保育、烹饪和清洁、预算管理以及与其他家庭关系的日常维护（即家庭关系）。 社会资本）。

将在家的配偶转移到劳动力市场，她提供的每项服务都必须从最新的私募股权投资中回购（托儿所、外卖、清洁工、安全系统……）。 GDP 将每次购买记录为增长。 因此，在消费相同服务的情况下，一个家庭可以被衡量为更加富裕——只是现在他们在一起的时间更少了。

教育也是一样。 证书变得越来越强制性，也越来越具有破坏性。 这就是罗布·亨德森的分析确实没有切中要害的地方：如果上大学的年轻人比例大幅增加——从而损失了生命中的岁月并积累了创纪录的债务——而收入却没有大幅增加，这对年轻人来说确实是一个非常糟糕的兆头。

在职业许可和学位筛选激增的服务经济中，现在几乎所有充满活力的行业都需要学士学位。 我曾经在一家公司工作，该公司的创始人大声疾呼不需要学位！ 在实践中，根据我面试以及后来面试和雇用其他人的经验，该公司绝对需要学位（事实上，它需要最好的学位，并且著名的研究生学习是可取的）。

自 1990 年以来，公立大学的实际学杂费大约增加了两倍，自 1970 年以来增加了约五倍；自 2007 年以来，毕业时的平均债务实际增长了 41%，学生债务总额现已接近 1.8 万亿美元。 对于很大一部分借款人来说，工资溢价永远不会抵消余额，债务的作用纯粹是对家庭形成的征税：它提高了首付的储蓄门槛，推迟结婚，推迟或取消孩子的赎回权。

在高等教育之下，学校层面也存在同样的回购动力。 公立学校是继承公地中最有价值的组成部分之一：也许并不完美，但通常是一个自由、有序、当地值得信赖的机构，一个家庭可以通过其地址获得。 在它已经陷入混乱的地方——市区的表现和安全数据毫无疑问地表明，在许多地方它已经陷入混乱——家庭面临着艰难的选择。

由于修女的缺乏，旧时廉价而有效的天主教学校系统已基本崩溃。 因此，选择是支付私人学费（自 1973 年以来实际增长了约 158%），支付功能性学校学区的住房溢价，或者通过家庭教育支付父母劳动费用，并放弃调整更广泛经济的第二收入。 “好学区”溢价是社会资本金融化并作为房地产转售的一个特别纯粹的例子。

就业波动和职业市场的变化使这一切变得更加复杂。 收入难以预测的年轻人理性地希望在做出承诺之前有更大的私人缓冲（储蓄、证书、获得罕见的稳定职业……）。 在此背景下，住房、教育和儿童保育的通货膨胀使得这种缓冲变得极其难以实现。

年轻人被告知他们变得更富有了，从平面电视的角度来看，他们确实更富有了。 但构成生活的物品——房子、配偶、孩子、好学校、好邻居——已经远远超出了工资水平，以至于 1965 年的普通生活道路现在需要非凡的收入。 事实证明，我们确实需要社会资本。 他们过得不好！

- 约翰
